questions.scenario:
The orders container starts successfully and then application code uses the AWS SDK to retrieve the current database credential. The call fails with:

```log
AccessDeniedException: assumed-role/orders-api-task-role is not authorized to perform secretsmanager:GetSecretValue on orders/prod/database-url
```

Which access path should the team inspect first?

options.text: Access: inspect only the secret resource policy, even though the denial names the task role's missing action.
Inspect the task execution role used by the ECS agent, even though this denied SDK call names the application task role.
Inspect the application task role, the denied action and secret resource, and whether this task should read that secret.
Access: attach the managed SecretsManagerReadWrite policy to the task role to restore service quickly.

questions.explanation:
Application code made this Secrets Manager call after the container started, and the denial names the task-role session. The task role therefore needs narrowly scoped permission to the required secret. The task execution role is the relevant identity when the ECS agent injects a referenced secret before startup, not when application code calls the AWS API at runtime. Broad Secrets Manager administration permission would exceed the workload's requirement.

Learn more in [Amazon ECS task IAM role](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/task-iam-roles.html).

questions.scenario: When a receipt image lands in an S3 bucket, DevPolaris needs a small job to resize it and write a thumbnail. The work is short, event-driven, and does not need a long-running web process. Which compute service fits best?

options.text: Run a continuously available Fargate worker that polls an SQS queue fed by S3 events.
Run the existing image-processing package on a small EC2 Auto Scaling group triggered through SQS.
Use an S3 Batch Operations job with Lambda only after collecting uploads into scheduled batches.
Lambda, because a small function can run when the upload event arrives.

questions.explanation:
Lambda matches a small event-driven job like reacting to an upload. ECS and EC2 can run workers, so they are plausible, but they add a long-running service or server model that the scenario does not need. DynamoDB stores key-based records and does not execute resize code.

Learn more in [AWS Lambda concepts](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/welcome.html).

questions.scenario:
A CI pipeline for DevPolaris deploys the orders API and fails while registering a task definition:

```log
User: arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/devpolaris-deploy-role is not authorized to perform iam:PassRole on arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/orders-api-task-role
```

Which explanation best fits the article's access model?

options.text: The deployment role is the caller, `iam:PassRole` is the action, and the task role is the resource being passed.
The deployment role can pass the task role, but the trust policy on the task role does not name ECS tasks.
The task execution role lacks permission to pull the image, so ECS reports the denial as `iam:PassRole`.
The deployment role lacks `ecs:RegisterTaskDefinition`, which IAM reports using the role referenced by the task definition.

questions.explanation:
IAM decisions can be read as caller, action, resource, and conditions. Here the deploy role lacks permission to pass the task role. The other options reuse real AWS nouns, but they do not match the actors or action named in the error.

Learn more in [Granting permissions to pass an IAM role](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles_use_passrole.html).

questions.scenario: The DevPolaris orders service had a one-zone incident. A review finds one app task, one subnet, no tested database restore, and no owner tag on the production resources. Which comments correctly use the cost and resilience part of the map?

options.text: Keep backups and test restore steps before treating the recovery plan as real.
Increase the desired task count in the same subnet and treat the database backup schedule as sufficient recovery evidence.
Run at least two app tasks across more than one Availability Zone for a better first production shape.
Add service, environment, and owner tags so cost and ownership are easier to trace.
Add a second Availability Zone for the tasks but keep the database, backups, and restore procedure unchanged.

questions.explanation:
The article's first resilience shape includes multiple tasks, multiple AZs, backups, restore testing, and useful tags for ownership and cost review. Removing logs is wrong because observability still gives evidence when resilience mechanisms fail or recover.

Learn more in [AWS disaster recovery options](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/wellarchitected/latest/reliability-pillar/plan-for-disaster-recovery-dr.html).

questions.scenario:
The finance export job logs `upload complete`, but finance cannot find the CSV. The log line says:

```text
bucket=devpolaris-orders-export
key=prod/2026-05/orders.csv
```

The documented bucket is `devpolaris-orders-exports`. What is the best next check?

options.text: Data and configuration: verify whether the application account has a second approved export bucket with the singular name.
Data and configuration: compare the bucket and key the app wrote with the expected S3 location.
Data and configuration: inspect S3 replication status before confirming which bucket and key the worker actually used.
Signals: search other task streams for the same request before treating this one successful upload line as authoritative.

questions.explanation:
The evidence shows a data-location mismatch: the app wrote to a similarly named bucket without the final `s`. Compute replacement, load balancer checks, and deleting logs are plausible distractions from other map boxes, but the log already gives the concrete storage clue.

Learn more in [Amazon S3 object keys](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/object-keys.html).

questions.scenario:
A customer opens `https://orders.devpolaris.com`. The intended path is:

Route 53 hosted zone is devpolaris.com. Alias target is dualstack.devpolaris-orders-alb-123.us-east-1.elb.amazonaws.com. ALB listener is HTTPS 443. Target group is devpolaris-orders-api-tg.

What does DNS prove in this path?

options.text: It proves the alias target currently resolves to an AWS load balancer name, but not that the listener or certificate is correct.
It proves Route 53 health evaluation considers the alias target available when evaluate-target-health is enabled.
It proves clients can resolve an address for the entry point, but not that security groups permit the connection.
It tells the browser which entry point the friendly name should reach.

questions.explanation:
DNS answers the location question: where should this name go? TLS answers the identity and trust question, and target health comes later in the path. Keeping those promises separate makes custom-domain debugging much easier.

Learn more in [Route 53 concepts](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/Route53/latest/DeveloperGuide/Welcome.html).

questions.scenario:
An engineer verifies the public name with hostname checking enabled:

```text
$ openssl s_client -connect orders.devpolaris.com:443 -servername orders.devpolaris.com -verify_hostname orders.devpolaris.com
subject=CN = orders.devpolaris.com
X509v3 Subject Alternative Name: DNS:orders.devpolaris.com
Verify return code: 0 (ok)
```

What does this evidence prove?

options.text: The server presented a certificate trusted for `orders.devpolaris.com` during the TLS handshake.
It proves the ALB selected the certificate whose subject matches the requested SNI name, but not that the client trusted its chain.
It proves the certificate chain was accepted, but hostname authorization still requires matching `orders.devpolaris.com` against the SAN.
It proves TCP and TLS negotiation reached a listener, while application and target health remain untested.

questions.explanation:
The explicit hostname check, matching SAN, and `Verify return code: 0` together show that the server presented a chain accepted for `orders.devpolaris.com`. SNI or a matching subject alone would not prove hostname validation, and this handshake still says nothing about the HTTP route, target health, or database path.

Learn more in [OpenSSL certificate verification](https://docs.openssl.org/master/man1/openssl-s_client/).
